message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>There is discussion evolving around the emergence of different phenotypes of Covid-19-associated thromboembolic disease, i.e. acute pulmonary embolism vs pulmonary thrombosis and different phenotypes of in situ thrombosis. With this study, we wish to provide hospitalization, treatment and in-hospital outcome data for pulmonary embolism during the 2020 Covid-19 pandemic and a corresponding 2016 – 2019 control period.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We performed a retrospective analysis of claims data of Helios hospitals in Germany. Consecutive cases with a hospital admission between January 1 and December 15, 2020 and pulmonary embolism as primary discharge diagnosis were analyzed and compared to a corresponding period covering the same weeks in 2016 – 2019.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    As previously reported for other emergent medical conditions, there was a hospitalization deficit coinciding with the 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    pandemic wave. Beginning with the 12-week interval May 6 – July 28, there was a stable surplus of hospital admissions in 2020. During this surplus period (May 6 – December 15, 2020), there were 2,449 hospitalizations including 45 PCR-confirmed Covid-19 cases (1.8%) as opposed to 8,717 in 2016 – 2019 (IRR 1.12, 95% CI 1.07 – 1.18,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.01). When excluding Covid-19 cases IRR was 1.10 (95% CI 1.05 – 1.15,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.01). While overall comorbidities expressed as weighted AHRQ Elixhauser Comorbidity Index (14.1 ± 10.1 vs. 13.9 ±10.3,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.28), the presence of thrombosis (46.1 vs 45.4%,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.55) and surgery (3.8 vs. 4.3%,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.33) were comparable, coagulopathy (3.3 vs 4.5%, P=0.01) and metastatic cancer (3.0 vs 4.0%,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.03) as contributing factors were less frequently observed during the 2020 surplus. Interventional treatments (thrombolytic therapy, thrombectomy or inferior vena cava filter placement) were less frequently used (4.7 vs 6.6%, OR 0.72, 95% CI 0.58 − 0.89,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    < 0.01). Similarly, intensive care (35.1 vs 38.8%, OR 0.83, 95% CI 0.75 − 0.92,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    < 0.01) and mechanical ventilation utilization (7.2 vs 8.1%, OR 0.88, 95% CI 0.74 – 1.04,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.14) as well as in-hospital-mortality rates (7.8 vs 9.8%, OR 0.76, 95% CI 0.64 − 0.90,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    < 0.01) were lower in 2020 compared with 2016 – 2019. This was associated with a shorter length of hospital stay (6.4 ±5.4 vs. 7.2 ±5.7 days,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    < 0.01) during the 2020 surplus period.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Only a minority of cases were associated with PCR-confirmed Covid-19 but this does not rule out preceding or undetected SARS-CoV-2 infection. Although there is a shift towards milder disease course, the increased incidence of hospitalizations for pulmonary embolism requires immediate attention, close surveillance and further studies.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Hospitalizations, resource use and outcomes of acute pulmonary embolism in Germany during the Covid-19 pandemic Emergence of different phenotypes of thrombotic disease?
